race.name: BP MS 150
race.tagline: 5,000+ riders crush 150 miles over two days for MS, Texas-style grit and glory.

vitals.location: Houston to College Station, Texas, USA
vitals.field_size: Over 5,000 riders swarm from Houston and Austin
vitals.registration: Tiered fees $100-$140 plus $400 fundraising minimum; includes T-shirt at $500, medal, jersey perks
vitals.feed_zones: Every 8-18 miles with hydration, snacks, mechanics, medical, SAG wagons
route_options: Houston Full: 100mi Day 1 + 80mi Day 2 — epic grind
Austin Full: 65-100mi Day 1 + 45-80mi Day 2 — scenic twist
Short Options: 38mi Bastrop route, Leap Ahead shortcuts

climate.description: Late April brings balmy 70-85°F temps with rising humidity that saps strength by midday. Expect possible light rain turning roads slick amid Gulf breezes. Nights cool to 60°F for recovery in La Grange tents.

terrain.surface: Smooth paved county roads wind through ranchlands and state parks. Fresh asphalt dominates with occasional chip-seal grit. Tree canopies shade Bastrop stretches amid open prairie exposure.

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 76. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 74.

course_description.character: The event has drifted well past its old 'Houston to Austin, 150 miles' identity. It's now officially Bike MS: ACC Texas MS 150 — BP dropped sponsorship — with multiple route combinations starting from Houston-area locations, Austin, or Bastrop, overnighting in La Grange, and finishing at Kyle Field, Texas A&M, in College Station. Total mileage depends on which route combination you pick; the longest two-day option runs roughly 178 miles, not a fixed 150. This is a charity ride on open public roads, not a closed-course race.
course_description.signature_challenge: There is no signature climb — Texas Hill Country rollers, not mountains. The real test is the distance-and-heat combination across two late-April days, plus navigating which of six-plus route options actually gets you to College Station.
suffering_zones: Houston Energy Corridor to La Grange (96 mi) — the longest single-day option
Buescher and Bastrop State Parks — tree cover breaks up otherwise open, exposed prairie riding
La Grange to College Station via Carmine (82 mi) — the closing day, arriving into the Kyle Field finish

biased_opinion.verdict: The Name Doesn't Match the Ride Anymore
biased_opinion.summary: This has been Texas's largest Bike MS fundraiser for four decades, but the name is legacy branding now: BP is no longer the title sponsor (it's officially Bike MS: ACC Texas MS 150), and it no longer runs the historic Houston-to-Austin route. Riders now choose from several Houston-, Austin-, or Bastrop-area starts, overnight in La Grange, and finish at Texas A&M's Kyle Field — with total distance varying by route combination rather than a fixed 150 miles. The 2026 edition drew more than 5,000 riders and raised over $9.2 million, still the largest event in the national Bike MS series, but well down from the roughly 13,000-rider peak of earlier decades.
strengths: The largest fundraising event in the national Bike MS series — $9.2M+ raised in 2026 for a real, longstanding cause
Four decades of organizational experience (founded 1985) and a genuinely large support infrastructure
Multiple route/distance options let riders of different fitness levels choose their day
A memorable finish inside Kyle Field at Texas A&M
weaknesses: Database entries or expectations built around 'Houston to Austin, 150 miles' are obsolete — the course and even the name have changed
Each cyclist carries a $400 fundraising minimum, separate from any registration fee
Point-to-point logistics require real planning: officially offered return shuttles cost $45-$85, and overnight tent reservations at La Grange run extra
Field size has declined substantially from the event's historic peak of roughly 13,000 riders to just over 5,000 in 2026
Roads remain open to traffic throughout — this is a charity ride, not a closed-course race
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A well-run, high-impact charity ride with real fundraising scale — but if you're picturing the old Houston-to-Austin 150-mile course, update your mental map. Come for the cause and the Kyle Field finish, not for a fixed 150-mile point-to-point.

final_verdict.one_liner: 5,000+ riders crush 150 miles over two days for MS, Texas-style grit and glory.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want to support the National MS Society through Texas's largest Bike MS fundraiser and can meet the $400 fundraising minimum. Know going in that the course is no longer a fixed Houston-to-Austin 150 miles — it's now Houston/Austin/Bastrop starts through La Grange to a Texas A&M finish.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want a similar-scale charity ride with fixed point-to-point distance: LoToJa Classic (Utah/Idaho/Wyoming). If you want the historic Houston-to-Austin distance specifically, note that course no longer exists in this event.

race_day_tips.text: Slow down and be careful during certain sections of the race.

additional_quotes.text: I can't believe they're letting us on the field - that is a hundred times better than Austin

rider_intel.search_text: The BP MS 150 from Houston to College Station receives positive feedback from riders, with one participant noting the race experience is significantly better than Austin events. The transcript suggests there are sections where riders need to exercise caution and slow down. Participants express surprise and appreciation for being allowed access to certain areas during the race, though specific course details and terrain conditions are not detailed in the available video content.

eligibility.notes: rebranded Bike MS: ACC Texas MS 150 (BP no longer sponsor); ran Apr 25-26 2026
